item.title: Team PNG ready for Commonwealth Games
item.description: Team PNG is ready to take its place on the international stage as the countdown ends for the opening of the Glasgow 2026 Commonwealth Games. The Opening Ceremony will be held at the OVO Hydro Arena on Thursday evening in Scotland, which is 5:00am Papua New Guinea time on Friday.
item.content:encoded:
Team PNG is ready to take its place on the international stage as the countdown ends for the opening of the Glasgow 2026 Commonwealth Games.

The Opening Ceremony will be held at the OVO Hydro Arena on Thursday evening in Scotland, which is 5:00am Papua New Guinea time on Friday.

Papua New Guinea will compete in boxing, weightlifting, women's 3x3 basketball, swimming and athletics.

Team PNG Chef de Mission Elaine Iorive said the athletes have worked hard to prepare for one of the biggest sporting events on the Commonwealth calendar.

"After months of training and preparation, Team PNG is proud and excited to be part of the Commonwealth Games Opening Ceremony. Marching behind our flag on the world stage is a special moment for every athlete and official," she said.

Ms Iorive said the team is ready to represent Papua New Guinea with pride and showcase the country's resilience, talent and sporting spirit.

"We are ready to represent Papua New Guinea with pride and to showcase the resilience, talent and spirit of our people throughout the Games."

Team PNG begins competitions on Friday in the women's 3x3 basketball, swimming and boxing.

The Glasgow 2026 Opening Ceremony will also make Commonwealth Games history as the first to be staged entirely inside an indoor arena.

item.title: PNG go down brave to New Zealand in OFC U16 semis
item.description: New Zealand have qualified for a 13th FIFA U-17 World Cup and secured progression to the final of OFC U-16 Men's Championship 2026 with a 3-0 win over Papua New Guinea at the PNG Football Stadium in Port Moresby. They move on to face Fiji in Saturday's title-decider at the same venue, overcoming a spirited display from the tournament hosts with three second half goals.
item.content:encoded:
New Zealand have qualified for a 13th FIFA U-17 World Cup and secured progression to the final of OFC U-16 Men's Championship 2026 with a 3-0 win over Papua New Guinea at the PNG Football Stadium in Port Moresby.

They move on to face Fiji in Saturday's title-decider at the same venue, overcoming a spirited display from the tournament hosts with three second half goals.

Papua New Guinea had the gloves of goalkeeper Luther Rodney to thank for keeping New Zealand frustrated through the first 45 minutes.

Twice he denied opposing captain Ben Trenberth, first from a one-one-position, and then from a header at the back post.

Both of New Zealand’s goals in their 2-0 win over Papua New Guinea at this same stage of the competition last year came in the second half, and this one followed a similar script.

The Junior All Whites let loose after the break.

Davi Valle Dos Santos provided the breakthrough in the 50th minute, squaring the ball to the back post, where Peitersen’s effort deflected off defender Bomecge Basanuc and into the bottom-left corner of the net.

Pietersen got his goal ten minutes later instead, meeting a cross into the box from Jack Clegg that he calmly tucked into the opposite corner.

It wasn't all one-way traffic though, and Will Martin's teammates were congratulating him when the New Zealand goalkeeper got up acrobatically to tip away a deflected shot from Papua New Guinea substitute Klivet Ronald.

Pietersen added the third goal in the dying moments of second half stoppage-time, getting in-behind the Papua New Guinea defence to round out a comfortable win for the Junior All Whites.

New Zealand will be chasing an 11th consecutive OFC U-16 Men's Championship title when they take to the field on Saturday, whilst Papua New Guinea will have one final chance to qualify for a first FIFA U-17 World Cup when they take on the Solomon Islands in the third place play-off.

New Zealand: 3 [Own Goal 50’, Cruz PIETERSEN 60’, 90+5') Papua New Guinea: 0

item.title: Parliament drives payroll reform
item.description: The transfer of the public service payroll system from the Department of Finance to the Department of Personnel Management [DPM] has been described as a major reform driven by parliamentary oversight following years of delays and serious weaknesses identified in an independent review. Chairman of the Parliamentary Committee on Public Sector Reform, Gary Juffa, said the launch shows that parliament
item.content:encoded:
The transfer of the public service payroll system from the Department of Finance to the Department of Personnel Management [DPM] has been described as a major reform driven by parliamentary oversight following years of delays and serious weaknesses identified in an independent review.

Chairman of the Parliamentary Committee on Public Sector Reform, Gary Juffa, said the launch shows that parliamentary committees can do more than simply table reports in Parliament.

"Today is testament to a Parliamentary Committee pushing its limits to drive accountability of public administration," Mr Juffa said.

"Public sector reform in this country must not merely be announced, but delivered. Not merely funded, but implemented. And not merely celebrated, but measured."

Mr Juffa acknowledged the Marape-Rosso Government for supporting parliamentary committees with the resources needed to carry out oversight work, even when those committees have been critical of the government.

He also reminded those attending that concerns over the public service payroll were first identified more than a decade ago.

Mr Juffa said the decision to conduct a payroll audit was originally made by the O'Neill Government in 2014, but it was not implemented until the Marape-Rosso Government took office in 2019 and ensured the review was carried out.

The independent Deloitte Payroll Diagnostic Review, completed in April 2023, uncovered 53 findings across the public service payroll system, with 27 classified as high-impact and high-priority, along with 135 recommendations for reform.

Among the major issues identified were deceased and terminated employees who remained on the payroll and continued receiving salaries, while some individual payments exceeding K100,000 in a single fortnight were processed without proper review.

Mr Juffa said Deloitte also identified a serious conflict of interest because the Department of Finance was responsible for using, administering and paying through the payroll system.

"The Department of Finance was, at one and the same time, a user of the payroll system, the administrator of the payroll system, and the payer of every public servant in this nation."

He said Deloitte concluded that the concentration of those responsibilities meant existing controls over the payroll process had "little or no effect at all."

The report recommended that payroll administration be returned to the Department of Personnel Management under the Public Service Management Act, with the establishment of a dedicated public service payroll office.

Mr Juffa noted that while the recommendation has now been implemented, it took three years and three months after Deloitte submitted its report.

"Reform delayed is money lost and it is trust lost."

He cautioned that simply transferring payroll responsibilities would not solve the problem unless strong internal controls, regular audits and proper segregation of duties are maintained within DPM.

"Moving the keys does not remove the risk, it moves it."

Mr Juffa said Parliament will continue monitoring the reform and expects DPM to report back within six months on progress made in removing ghost employees, cleaning payroll records, reviewing user access and upgrading the ALESCO payroll system.

item.title: Opposition calls for immediate swearing-in of new Electoral Commissioner
item.description: Opposition Leader James Nomane has urged the Prime Minister and the Chief Secretary to immediately ensure that Papua New Guinea's newly appointed Electoral Commissioner, Professor Nicole Haley, is sworn into office. In a statement, Mr. Nomane said Professor Haley was appointed by the Electoral Commission's Appointments Committee three weeks ago but has yet to assume office.
item.content:encoded:
Opposition Leader James Nomane has urged the Prime Minister and the Chief Secretary to immediately ensure that Papua New Guinea's newly appointed Electoral Commissioner, Professor Nicole Haley, is sworn into office.

In a statement, Mr. Nomane said Professor Haley was appointed by the Electoral Commission's Appointments Committee three weeks ago but has yet to assume office.

He said the issue of writs for the 2027 National General Election is less than nine months away, and her delay in taking office is increasing the risks to election preparations and its successful conduct.

Mr. Nomane said the process of appointing Professor Haley was straightforward and uncontested and should have taken only a few days to complete, not weeks.

He also called on the National Government to introduce a Supplementary Budget during the next sitting of Parliament to ensure adequate funding and timely preparations for the 2027 National General Election.

When contacted, Prime Minister James Marape said the Government is waiting for the Salaries and Remuneration Commission to finalise Professor Haley's contract before she officially takes office.

He added that it is business as usual at the Papua New Guinea Electoral Commission in the meantime.

item.title: Nine-Mile eviction victims seek justice during Minister’s visit
item.description: Families evicted from Portion 11, 55 & 56 at 9 Mile have called for an investigation into the ownership of the land, saying they have lived on the property for more than four decades. The call was made during a visit by newly appointed Minister for Environment and Climate Change and Member for Port Moresby North East, Hon. John Kaupa.
item.content:encoded:
Families evicted from Portion 11, 55 & 56 at 9 Mile have called for an investigation into the ownership of the land, saying they have lived on the property for more than four decades.

The call was made during a visit by newly appointed Minister for Environment and Climate Change and Member for Port Moresby North East, Hon. John Kaupa.

He also delivered food rations to the displaced families and assured them of his support.

Minister Kaupa acknowledged the hardship faced by the affected residents but said the eviction was carried out under a lawful court order that must be respected.

“It is too late for anything now. We are all under the law and must follow the court order,” he said.

The Minister assured the families that his office would work towards resettling them on newly titled land and encouraged people living in settlements on State land across Port Moresby North-East to visit his electorate office for assistance in securing legal land titles in the future.

Community representative Ruth Kumun said the affected families have lived on the land for more than 40 years and are demanding transparency over how the land titles were obtained.

She called on Minister Kaupa to support the community by pushing for an investigation into the land acquisition process.

Ms. Kumun also alleged that residents were not served with eviction notices and were not given adequate time to relocate before the eviction was carried out.

She claimed the eviction was conducted swiftly, leaving many families without enough time to remove their belongings or make alternative living arrangements.

The affected residents are now appealing to the Government and relevant authorities to investigate their concerns while also seeking assistance to rebuild their lives following the eviction.

item.title: Sungi denies allegation of locking out Minister Kaupa
item.description: The Office of Climate Change and Development Authority [CCDA] has denied allegations that they were responsible for locking out the new Minister for Environment and Conservation, John Kaupa, early this week. Acting CCDA Managing Director, Debra Sungi in a statement, says that she nor any of her CCDA Officers, has access to, or possession of the Minister's office and keys.
item.content:encoded:
The Office of Climate Change and Development Authority [CCDA] has denied allegations that they were responsible for locking out the new Minister for Environment and Conservation, John Kaupa, early this week.

Acting CCDA Managing Director, Debra Sungi in a statement, says that she nor any of her CCDA Officers, has access to, or possession of the Minister's office and keys.

Ms. Sungi said that the Minis terial Office is a political office, that is allocated to the Minister responsible, and it has separate administrative functions to the CCDA, even though they are both in the same premises.

She added that CCDA had initially planned to facilitate the Ministerial handover takeover on Wednesday July 22nd, but there was some uncertainty, when there were some public announcement's that the Forest Ministry will also oversee the Climate Change portfolio.

Ms. Sungi further added that, this prompted them to await the official Ministerial Determination and Gazettal Notice, before they proceed with the ministerial changes.

The Minister's First Secretary, Jack Ninkama told the media yesterday that the office was locked and the keys could not be located.

The media was also told by Mr. Ninkama that he has instructed the building Manager to change the locks to the Ministers Office, so his staff can gain access to the building, and prepare the place for the scheduled handover takeover ceremony as earlier scheduled.

item.title: Operation ‘Rausim Illegal Gambling’ nets 65 arrests in WHP and Jiwaka
item.description: A major crackdown on illegal gambling in Western Highlands and Jiwaka has resulted in the arrest of 65 people, who are now being held at the Mt Hagen Police Station cell block. The operation was carried out last Friday by police personnel from Jiwaka and Western Highlands in collaboration with the National Gaming Control Board (NGCB).
item.content:encoded:
A major crackdown on illegal gambling in Western Highlands and Jiwaka has resulted in the arrest of 65 people, who are now being held at the Mt Hagen Police Station cell block.

The operation was carried out last Friday by police personnel from Jiwaka and Western Highlands in collaboration with the National Gaming Control Board (NGCB).

The joint operation aimed to eliminate all gambling activities that are not authorised by the regulator, the NGCB.

Illegal gambling hotspots targeted during the operation included Mt Hagen's infamous Okapana Market along the Okuk Highway near Dobel, the Animal Market opposite Rainbow Supermarket in town, and several other locations in Jiwaka.

During the operation, police confiscated gambling machines, compressors, generators, mobile phones, tablets and other equipment used to operate illegal High-Low and Ludoo gambling games.

Those arrested include vendors and operators accused of running illegal gambling businesses.

Speaking at a media conference on Monday, Western Highlands Provincial Police Commander Chief Superintendent John Sagom said police will continue to enforce the law against illegal gambling operators.

"We have removed and confiscated the tools used to run these illegal gambling activities. These operators have been warned before, and now we are taking action. We will not allow illegal gambling to continue because it contributes to law and order problems in our communities," Chief Superintendent Sagom said.

PPC Sagom said the Gaming Control Act 2007 clearly states that any gambling activity operating without a licence is illegal.

He said the NGCB has spent several years conducting public awareness campaigns across the country, leaving operators with no excuse to claim they were unaware of the law.

"The National Gaming Control Board has been educating the public since 2019 about legal and illegal gambling. People should already know which gambling activities are licensed and which are not. Anyone operating without a licence is committing an offence and will face the full force of the law," he said.

Meanwhile, the 65 suspects are expected to be formally charged with operating illegal gambling activities.

Under the Gaming Control Act 2007, an individual convicted of operating illegal gambling faces a fine of K5,000 or up to 12 months' imprisonment, while companies may be fined K10,000 or face up to 12 months' imprisonment.

The Act states that only four forms of gambling are legally authorised in Papua New Guinea—casinos, gaming machines (pokies), sports betting and lotteries, including bingo.

High-Low and Ludoo are not gazetted under the Act and are therefore illegal.

The NGCB says it conducted nationwide awareness campaigns on illegal gambling from 2019 to 2025, and the current operation forms part of its ongoing efforts to reduce the social and economic impacts of illegal gambling across the country.

Chief Superintendent Sagom warned that police will continue conducting similar operations throughout Western Highlands.

"This operation will continue. I am warning all illegal gambling operators to stop now. Do not ignore the awareness that has been given. Comply with the law or be prepared to face arrest and prosecution," Sagom said.

item.title: Agiru memorial school in Tari receives STEM Kits to boost science learning
item.description: Agiru Memorial Dauli High School in Komo Hulia District has received a major boost to science education after collecting three crates of science kits donated by the National Department of Education [NDoE] from the Hela Education Services Office in Tari on Tuesday [21-07-26]. The school was among four Hela high schools selected for the donation, alongside Juni, Pureni, and Paka High Schools. The su
item.content:encoded:
Agiru Memorial Dauli High School in Komo Hulia District has received a major boost to science education after collecting three crates of science kits donated by the National Department of Education [NDoE] from the Hela Education Services Office in Tari on Tuesday [21-07-26].

The school was among four Hela high schools selected for the donation, alongside Juni, Pureni, and Paka High Schools. The support followed a formal request by Hela Governor Philip Undialu to the NDoH.

School Principal Terence Tandale thanked the Governor for recognizing Dauli High as one of the province’s future Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics [STEM] institutions.

“I acknowledge the Governor for recognizing our school and providing three crates of science kits for Grades 7 to 10 students,” Mr. Tandale said.

He said education services in Hela have improved significantly since former Hela Education Services Director Ronny Angu took office. He is now the Provincial Administrator.

“With the support of the provincial government, education services have improved significantly. Donations like this will ensure student learning is improved,” he said.

Mr. Tandale said the kits are vital because they move students beyond textbooks and abstract theory into practical, hands-on learning.

“By allowing students to touch, mix, build and observe, these kits improve concept retention, encourage independent problem-solving, and inspire long-term interest in critical STEM fields,” he explained.

“For students, especially in under-resourced or rural schools, these kits turn real-world science into visible reality. Instead of just memorizing definitions, learners are involved in the ‘tell, show, experience’ model. That deepens comprehension and makes education fun.

Senior Science Teacher Parabu Peterson said this is the third donation the school has received from NDoE and the Hela Provincial Government.

He thanked both the National Department of Education and the provincial government for their continued support.

However, he said the school’s biggest need now is a dedicated science laboratory to properly store the equipment and conduct experiments.

“While we thank Governor Undialu for his continuous support, we are calling on the Provincial Government, the Komo Hulia District Development Authority, and other agencies to assist with funding a science laboratory,” Mr. Peterson appealed.

item.title: PNG, Australia reaffirms commitment to strengthen law and justice sector
item.description: Senior law and justice leaders from Papua New Guinea have reaffirmed their commitment to work together to improve community safety, strengthen access to justice and deliver better services for citizens.

item.title: Communities in Simbu experiencing full impact of drought
item.description: Simbu Province is staring down a humanitarian crisis as El Niño enters its second month, with drinking water sources drying up, food gardens failing, and bushfires destroying crops and forest.
